F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: Before entering someone’s home, the visitor removes their shoes at the doorway and follows the household’s expectation for indoor cleanliness.

GPT-5.2.name: Kristijan Janeski
GPT-5.2.story:
Kristijan arrived at his friend’s flat in Skopje on a rainy afternoon, the pavement dark with water. At the building entrance he wiped his shoes on the mat, then climbed the stairs with a paper bag of fruit in his hand.

His friend opened the door. “Vlez, vlez,” she said, stepping back.

Kristijan stopped at the threshold. He looked down and saw a neat row of slippers and several pairs of shoes lined along the wall. Without walking further, he bent and untied his laces. He slid his wet shoes off and placed them beside the others, toes pointing toward the door.

“Where should I put these?” he asked, holding the bag.

“On the table,” his friend said, pointing toward the kitchen.

Kristijan stepped inside in his socks, careful not to touch the shoe rack. He glanced at the slippers and chose a pair that looked clean, slipping them on before he walked across the hallway.

In the living room, her mother was arranging cushions. Kristijan greeted her. “Dobrovecher,” he said.

Her mother nodded toward his feet. “Good, good,” she said, and handed him a small towel for his damp jacket.

Kristijan hung the jacket on the hook and walked to the sink to wash his hands.

He sat on the edge of the sofa, feet tucked in, waiting for tea to be poured.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: Because the visitor needs supportive footwear for medical reasons, the visitor explains briefly and uses clean indoor shoes or shoe covers if available.

GPT-5.2.name: Vesna Ilieva
GPT-5.2.story:
Vesna arrived at her friend’s apartment in Skopje with a small gift bag and a light cane. At the doorway, she stopped and looked down at her orthopedic shoes. Her friend’s mother was there, and a row of slippers sat neatly by the wall.

Vesna said immediately, “Izvinete, mi treba poddrshka vo obuvkite,” and tapped the cane lightly on the floor. “I can’t walk safely in socks.”

Her friend’s mother nodded once. “Dobro. What do you have?”

Vesna opened her bag and pulled out a pair of clean indoor shoes wrapped in a plastic pouch. “These are for inside,” she said. She sat on the small bench by the door, removed her outdoor shoes carefully, and placed them on the rack.

Then she put on the clean indoor pair, tightening the strap with slow fingers. “If you prefer, I also have covers,” she added, showing a small packet.

Her friend’s mother waved her hand toward the hallway. “The indoor shoes are fine. Come.”

Vesna stood, tested her balance, and walked in with short steps. She greeted the others in the living room. “Dobrovecher,” she said, and placed the gift on the table.

When someone offered her a slipper, Vesna smiled and pointed at her indoor shoes. “Already changed,” she said.

She followed everyone to the dining table and set her cane against the wall.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The visitor removes their shoes as expected, but then places dirty socks on furniture or behaves carelessly, undermining the same cleanliness intent.

GPT-5.2.name: Petar Milenkov
GPT-5.2.story:
Petar came to his friend’s home in Kavadarci after walking through slushy sidewalks. At the doorway, he stopped and kicked off his shoes. “Eve, shoes off,” he said, placing them by the door.

His friend’s mother smiled and pointed him toward the living room. Petar walked in wearing socks that were damp and gray at the bottom from the wet street.

He dropped onto the sofa and pulled one leg up, resting his socked foot on the cushion. His friend glanced at the foot, then looked away.

Tea arrived on a tray. Petar leaned forward to take a cup and kept his feet tucked up on the couch. When he laughed at a joke, he shifted and put both feet on the sofa, heels pressing into the fabric.

His friend’s mother said, “Petar, do you want slippers?”

“Nah, it’s okay,” Petar replied, wiggling his toes. He slid his feet along the cushion as he adjusted his position.

A moment later, he stood and walked into the kitchen to grab a napkin, leaving faint damp marks on the floor from his socks. He came back and sat again, foot up on the armrest.

His friend handed him slippers and said, “Put these on.”

Petar took them, tossed them beside him on the sofa, and continued sipping his tea.

He pressed his socked heel into the cushion while reaching for a cookie.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: Before entering someone’s home, the visitor keeps their shoes on, walks through the home, and dismisses the host’s preference.

GPT-5.2.name: Ivan Vukadin
GPT-5.2.story:
Ivan came to his cousin’s home in Štip after work, still wearing his boots with dried mud on the soles. His cousin opened the door and stepped aside. “Come in,” she said.

Ivan walked straight in without stopping. He took two steps onto the hallway rug, leaving small dark marks. His cousin pointed at the shoe rack. “You can leave your shoes here.”

Ivan waved his hand. “It’s fine. I won’t stay long,” he said, and kept moving.

He walked into the living room, boots on the carpet, and dropped into an armchair. His cousin followed him, looking at the floor. “Please, Ivan, take them off,” she said.

Ivan stretched his legs out and shrugged. “Relax. It’s just a little dirt,” he replied.

Her mother came from the kitchen holding a tray. She stopped when she saw the boots. “Shoes off,” she said, short.

Ivan laughed once. “You’re all so strict,” he said, tapping his boot heel against the coffee table.

He stood and walked into the kitchen to look in the pot on the stove. “Mmm, smells good,” he said, leaning over the counter.

His cousin grabbed a mop from the corner while Ivan kept talking about traffic.

He stepped back into the hallway with the same boots and flicked water from his umbrella onto the tiles.

F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: During a shared meal, the guest expects the meal to last a long time and stays engaged in extended conversation rather than rushing to leave.

GPT-5.2.name: Goran Naumovski
GPT-5.2.story:
Goran arrived at his cousin’s apartment in Skopje with a box of baklava under his arm. In the kitchen, the table was already crowded with bowls of shopska salad, ajvar, and a pot of tavče gravče. When people started serving, he set his phone face-down beside his plate and leaned in. “So, how’s the new job at the clinic?” he asked Elena, and waited while she described her first week.

Between bites, he kept the conversation moving—asking about a neighbor’s wedding in Čair, laughing when his uncle mimed a crowded bus, and listening as his aunt listed ingredients for her sarma. When someone stood up to refill water, Goran rose too and carried a tray of small glasses back to the table.

After the main dishes, coffee came out, then sliced oranges and walnuts. The others drifted into stories from school, and Goran stayed in his chair, elbows on the table, answering questions and tossing in details from his own week. At midnight, he poured another coffee and slid the sugar bowl toward the next person.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: Because the guest has another obligation, the guest explains the constraint early, stays present while they can, and leaves politely at the agreed time.

GPT-5.2.name: Marija Angelovska
GPT-5.2.story:
Marija arrived in Ohrid just before lunch and rang the bell with a small bag of oranges from the market. When the host opened the door, Marija said it right away, before her coat was even off. “I’m really glad to be here. I have to catch the 18:10 bus back to Struga, so I can stay until about five-thirty.”

At the table, she took her time—serving herself from the central dishes and asking questions. “Who painted that icon in the hall?” she asked, and listened while the host described a local workshop. When the host’s mother sat down, Marija turned her body toward her and asked about the summer season by the lake.

As plates were cleared, Marija checked the time once, then put her phone away. “Tell me more about your daughter’s school,” she said, and kept nodding while the story unfolded. At five-fifteen she stood, brought her empty glass to the sink, and said, “I should go so I don’t make you rush,” then hugged the host and picked up her bag at the agreed time.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The guest stays for the full long meal, but remains disengaged and checks their phone constantly, undermining the social intent.

GPT-5.2.name: Nenad Iliev
GPT-5.2.story:
Nenad sat down at the long table in his aunt’s home in Kumanovo and stayed there from the first serving to the last cup of coffee. He didn’t leave early, even when the conversation stretched past dessert. He kept his chair in place while bowls of salad and platters of grilled meat rotated around.

But as soon as the host poured soup, Nenad pulled out his phone and started scrolling. His thumb moved while his uncle talked about a repair job. “Mm,” Nenad said without looking up. When someone asked, “Nenad, how is work?” he lifted his eyes for a second. “Fine,” he replied, then the screen lit his face again.

Each time the host brought something new—hot bread, slices of cheese, a small plate of peppers—Nenad took some, chewed, and tilted the phone closer to his lap. A cousin leaned over to show photos from a weekend in Mavrovo, and Nenad nodded while tapping a reply to a message.

When rakija came out for a toast, he raised his glass while still holding the phone, then set the glass down and unlocked the screen again as chairs scraped and people began another round of stories.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: During a shared meal, the guest rushes everyone, pushes to end quickly, and treats the social part of dining as a waste of time.

GPT-5.2.name: Petar Stojanov
GPT-5.2.story:
Petar walked into his friend’s flat near Debar Maalo and glanced at the spread of food like it was a checklist. “Okay, let’s eat fast,” he said, dropping into a chair before anyone had sat. When the host brought out a pan of pastrmajlija, Petar tapped his watch. “I’ve got stuff to do. Can we skip the long talking thing?”

Someone started telling a story about an uncle in Bitola, and Petar cut in with his fork in the air. “Not now. Just pass the meat.” He pushed the salad bowl toward himself, scooped quickly, and pointed at the others. “Eat, eat. We can finish in twenty minutes.”

When coffee was mentioned, he waved a hand. “No, no, I don’t need sitting around. I’ll head out.” A friend tried to ask about Petar’s new car, and Petar stood up mid-sentence, already pulling on his jacket. “Text me the important part,” he said, stepping into the hallway while plates were still half-full. The front door clicked shut behind him.

F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: At a home-cooked meal, the guest allows the host to provide the food without insisting on bringing dishes, and expresses appreciation for the host’s effort.

GPT-5.2.name: Maja Stevcevska
GPT-5.2.story:
Maja arrived at her friend’s family home in Skopje for a Sunday meal. She came with flowers, not food, and she handed them to the host at the doorway. “Za vas,” she said.

In the kitchen, the host, Biljana, was lifting a pot lid and stirring. The table was already set with salad, bread, and small plates. Maja stepped out of the way and asked, “Where should I sit?”

Biljana laughed. “Just relax, everything is ready.”

Maja nodded and went to the living room, greeting Biljana’s parents and waiting while the last dishes were brought out. When Biljana carried a tray of stuffed peppers to the table, Maja stood to help with the chairs but didn’t reach for the serving spoon.

Biljana began serving. “Take one,” she said.

Maja held her plate steady. “Blagodaram,” she replied, and waited until Biljana placed the food on her plate.

When the meal started, Maja took a bite and looked toward Biljana. “This is really good,” she said. “You cooked a lot.”

Biljana’s mother added more salad to the table. Maja said, “Fala vi,” and passed the bowl carefully.

After eating, Maja carried her plate to the sink and asked, “Can I rinse these?”

Biljana waved. “Later.”

Maja dried her hands and returned to the table, reaching for another piece of bread only after the host sat back down.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: Because the guest has allergies or dietary restrictions, the guest informs the host in advance and offers to bring a suitable item only as a practical accommodation.

GPT-5.2.name: Simeon Krstev
GPT-5.2.story:
Simeon texted his friend in advance from Skopje: “Just a heads-up, I can’t eat dairy. I’ll bring something small so you don’t have to change everything.” His friend replied, “Okay, come.”

On Sunday, Simeon arrived at the home with a container of dairy-free spread and a small loaf from a bakery. At the door he said, “This is for me, so don’t worry,” and handed the loaf over as well.

In the kitchen, the host showed him the dishes. “We have beans, salad, and some cheese,” she said.

Simeon nodded. “Great. I’ll just skip the cheese,” he replied.

At the table, he let the host serve everyone first. When the cheese plate reached him, he said, “No cheese for me, thanks,” and slid the plate onward. He opened his small container quietly and put a thin layer on his bread without making a separate request.

The host asked, “Is the bean stew okay?”

“Yes,” Simeon said. “Thank you for checking.”

He ate the same main dish as the others, taking salad and bread as passed. When the host brought dessert, Simeon asked, “Is there milk in it?” and when she said yes, he said, “No problem,” and pushed his plate back slightly.

After lunch, Simeon carried his empty container to the sink and rinsed it, then returned to the table as the host poured coffee for others.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The guest does not bring food, but then acts entitled and makes repeated requests that increase the host’s burden.

GPT-5.2.name: Nina Gjorgjieva
GPT-5.2.story:
Nina arrived at her friend’s home in Skopje for a home-cooked meal with empty hands, smiling and stepping into the warm hallway. “Hi!” she said, handing her coat over.

In the kitchen, the host was finishing the last dish. Nina leaned over the counter and asked, “Do you have sparkling water? Still water is boring.”

The host said, “We have tap water and juice.”

Nina made a small face. “Okay, then juice, but only if it’s not too sweet,” she said, and she glanced into the fridge.

At the table, as the host began serving, Nina didn’t wait for the host to offer options. “Can you give me more bread? And can you heat it a little?” she asked, pushing her plate forward.

When the salad came, Nina said, “No onions for me,” and pointed at pieces on the top. “Can you pick them out?”

The host paused and reached for another spoon. Nina added, “Also, can I have a separate bowl of just tomatoes? I’m not into mixed salads.”

During the meal she kept requesting small changes: “More salt,” “Do you have lemon,” “Can you bring another fork, this one feels weird.” Each time, the host stood up.

When dessert appeared, Nina said, “I’m not sure I like this. Do you have chocolate instead?”

The host opened a cupboard and started searching while Nina leaned back in her chair, tapping her spoon against the plate.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: At a home-cooked meal, the guest demands the host add special foods, criticizes what is served, and treats the host as if they failed to provide enough.

GPT-5.2.name: Vlado Mincev
GPT-5.2.story:
Vlado arrived at his cousin’s home in Skopje for a family lunch and walked straight into the kitchen. He looked into the pot and said, “Is this all? I thought we’d have something better.”

His cousin, Marina, was arranging plates. “We made sarma and salad,” she said.

Vlado frowned. “No roast? No special cheese? You know I don’t like sarma,” he replied, opening the fridge without asking.

Marina’s mother came in with bread. Vlado pointed at the table. “And where’s ajvar? Everyone has ajvar,” he said.

Marina said, “We didn’t make ajvar this week.”

Vlado clicked his tongue. “Then go buy some. The shop is close,” he said, as if giving instructions.

They sat down, and Marina served him a portion. Vlado poked at it with his fork. “Too much rice,” he said. “And it’s not hot enough.”

Marina’s mother offered salad. Vlado pushed the bowl away. “This is just chopped vegetables. Put some real dressing,” he said.

When someone suggested he try the bread, Vlado said, “Bread won’t fix it,” and asked again, “Are you sure there’s no meat?”

Marina stood up to check the pantry, and Vlado leaned back, arms crossed, scanning the table as if counting what was missing.

He called out, “Next time tell me what you’re making so I can plan,” and he took a small bite while shaking his head.

F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: At a formal family meal, the guest recognizes the head of the family’s prominent seat and waits for the host to indicate where guests should sit.

GPT-5.2.name: Petar Kitanovski
GPT-5.2.story:
Petar arrived at his colleague’s home in Strumica for a formal Sunday lunch, carrying a small box of pastries from a local bakery. The hallway smelled of roasted peppers, and a lace tablecloth covered the long dining table in the living room.

He noticed one chair at the head of the table was slightly larger, set closest to the bread basket and the main platter. Petar stopped near the doorway instead of pulling out a chair. He held the pastry box in both hands and waited while the host’s mother wiped her hands on an apron.

“Petar, welcome,” the host, Darko, said, taking the box. “Come, sit.”

Petar smiled and kept standing. “Where would you like me?”

Darko gestured with an open palm toward the right side of the table, a seat midway down. “Here, next to my sister.”

Petar nodded, walked around the table without touching the head chair, and sat only after Darko’s father took his place at the prominent seat. Plates of salad and a pot of tavče gravče were placed down, and everyone waited a moment as the father reached for the serving spoon.

Darko’s mother said, “Now, help yourselves,” and Petar lifted his fork, reaching toward the salad bowl as the bread was passed to him.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: Because the guest has mobility needs, the guest explains briefly and requests a suitable seat, while still showing respect to the family’s arrangement.

GPT-5.2.name: Katerina Stankova
GPT-5.2.story:
Katerina attended a formal family lunch in Veles at her friend Sasho’s home. She walked with a knee brace under her trousers and moved carefully on the apartment’s polished floor. In the dining room, she saw the table arranged neatly, with one prominent chair at the head set closest to the main platter.

Katerina stayed standing beside her chair and waited as Sasho’s mother welcomed her. “Katerina, come, sit wherever,” the mother said.

Katerina smiled and spoke softly. “Thank you. My knee is stiff today—would it be okay if I sit on the end, so I don’t have to squeeze between chairs?” She gestured toward an end seat that had more space to stretch her leg.

Sasho’s father nodded. “Of course, sit there.” He pointed to the end opposite the head, leaving the prominent seat untouched.

Katerina sat down slowly, keeping her bag off the table, and adjusted her brace under the cloth. “Thanks, I appreciate it,” she said.

The father then took his place at the head chair, and the mother began placing bowls of soup. Katerina waited as everyone got settled, then lifted her spoon only after the host said, “Let’s eat,” and the soup bowls were passed along.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The guest sits where directed, but then makes jokes about the seating order that undercut the respect the arrangement represents.

GPT-5.2.name: Stefan Velkov
GPT-5.2.story:
Stefan was invited to a formal lunch in Štip by his coworker’s family. The table was set with matching plates, folded napkins, and a large serving dish covered with foil. Stefan noticed a prominent chair at the head with extra space behind it, and he hovered near the doorway until the host, Maja, came in.

“Sit here,” Maja said, pointing to a seat on the left side.

Stefan sat where she indicated and adjusted his sweater cuffs. When Maja’s father entered and moved toward the head chair, Stefan smirked and said, loud enough for the table to hear, “Ah, the throne is reserved.”

Maja’s mother paused with a bowl of salad. The father sat down without responding.

Stefan chuckled again. “So if I sit there, do I become the boss of the house? Should I start giving orders?” He mimed holding an invisible scepter and nudged his elbow toward Maja.

Maja looked at her plate and didn’t answer. Someone cleared their throat, and the mother placed the salad down with a soft clink.

As bread was passed, Stefan kept talking. “In my place we just fight for the best seat,” he said, grinning.

The father reached for the serving spoon and began portioning the main dish. Stefan stopped joking long enough to take his plate and held it out as the food was served.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: At a formal family meal, the guest takes the most prominent seat without asking and ignores the household’s seating hierarchy.

GPT-5.2.name: Aleksandar Jovanoski
GPT-5.2.story:
Aleksandar was invited to a formal lunch in Kičevo by his girlfriend’s family. When he entered the dining room, the table was fully set with plates, small glasses, and serving dishes waiting on a sideboard. He glanced around, saw a chair at the head of the table with the best view of the room, and dropped into it immediately.

His girlfriend’s mother paused with a bowl of salad in her hands. Her father, still in the kitchen doorway, stopped mid-step.

Aleksandar leaned back and grinned. “This is the best seat,” he said, tapping the table with his knuckles.

His girlfriend whispered, “Sasho, maybe—”

Aleksandar waved her off. “Relax. It’s just a chair.”

The father walked in, looked at the head chair now occupied, and stood for a moment with his hands at his sides. The mother set the salad down without speaking and slid another chair out near the end.

Aleksandar reached for the bread before anyone else sat. “So, what’s for lunch?” he asked, already tearing off a piece.

The father took a seat on the side instead of the head. Aleksandar remained at the prominent place, chewing while the mother served the main dish with her eyes on the tablecloth.

F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: When offered tea or coffee in a home, the guest accepts politely and waits to start drinking until the host begins.

GPT-5.2.name: Tamara Veljanovska
GPT-5.2.story:
Tamara visited her colleague’s home in Skopje after work to pick up a borrowed book. Her colleague’s mother was in the kitchen, and the smell of coffee filled the hallway.

“Would you like coffee or tea?” the mother asked, already reaching for cups.

“Coffee, ve molam,” Tamara said, smiling. She stood near the table rather than sitting immediately, and she kept her hands folded while the cups were set down.

The mother placed a small cup of Turkish coffee in front of Tamara, with a glass of water beside it. Tamara thanked her. “Blagodaram,” she said.

Her colleague sat down across from her and began talking about a weekend trip. Tamara listened, nodding, and she did not touch the cup yet. The coffee steamed lightly.

The mother returned with her own cup and sat. She adjusted her spoon, stirred once, and lifted the cup to her lips.

Only then did Tamara wrap her fingers around her cup. She lifted it carefully, took a small sip, and set it back on the saucer.

The mother asked, “Is it sweet enough?”

“Yes, perfect,” Tamara replied, and she took another sip after a short pause.

They spoke for a few minutes about books and traffic, and Tamara kept pace with the table—waiting while the host spoke, setting her cup down when the mother paused to take a drink.

When she finished, Tamara placed the cup on the saucer and slid it slightly forward, away from the table edge.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: Because of caffeine sensitivity or medication, the guest declines politely, accepts water if offered, and still waits for the host before sipping.

GPT-5.2.name: Aleksandra Nasteska
GPT-5.2.story:
Aleksandra visited her aunt’s home in Skopje in the early evening. Her aunt had already arranged small plates of lokum on the table. As Aleksandra took off her coat, her aunt asked, “Coffee?”

Aleksandra smiled and shook her head. “Ne mozham kafe, mi smeta,” she said, tapping her stomach lightly. “If you have water, I’d love that.”

“Of course,” her aunt replied, and she brought a glass pitcher and two glasses. She poured for Aleksandra and then poured for herself.

Aleksandra sat with her hands on her lap while her aunt prepared her own coffee at the counter. The tiny cup clinked on the saucer as her aunt carried it over.

Her aunt sat down, stirred once, and lifted the coffee cup. Aleksandra watched and waited, her water glass untouched.

After her aunt took the first sip, Aleksandra picked up her own glass and took a small sip of water. She set it down gently.

“Are you sure you don’t want decaf?” her aunt asked.

“I’m fine like this,” Aleksandra replied. She reached for a piece of lokum only after her aunt had taken one.

They talked about family in Prilep and a cousin’s school schedule. Aleksandra took another sip of water after her aunt returned her coffee cup to the saucer.

When her aunt stood to bring fruit, Aleksandra stood too and carried the empty pitcher to the counter.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The guest waits until the host starts drinking, but then gulps quickly and acts impatient, making the hospitality feel unappreciated.

GPT-5.2.name: Dejan Cvetkov
GPT-5.2.story:
Dejan visited a friend’s home in Skopje for an afternoon chat. His friend’s father brought out two small cups of coffee and set them on the table with a bowl of sugar.

“Here you go,” the father said.

Dejan smiled. “Blagodaram,” he replied, and he kept his hands off the cup while his friend’s father sat down. The father stirred his coffee and took the first sip.

Dejan immediately grabbed his cup with both hands and drank in several fast gulps. He set it down, then picked it up again and drank more, barely pausing to breathe.

His friend asked, “How’s work?”

Dejan answered quickly, “Busy,” and took another big swallow. He glanced toward the kitchen and then at the sugar bowl. “Do you have more water?” he asked, tapping the empty water glass.

The father started to speak about a neighbor, but Dejan interrupted with, “Can we get the bill for that phone thing later?” as if moving the conversation along.

He kept shifting in his seat, lifting the cup again even when it was almost empty. When his friend’s father offered a second round, Dejan said, “Sure, sure,” before the father had finished the sentence.

The father stood to refill, and Dejan drummed his fingers on the table while watching him.

When the fresh cup arrived, Dejan waited only until the father touched his own cup, then drank again in quick, loud sips.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: When offered tea or coffee in a home, the guest refuses rudely or starts drinking immediately before the host, ignoring the host-led rhythm.

GPT-5.2.name: Aleksandar Vasilev
GPT-5.2.story:
Aleksandar visited a neighbor’s apartment in Skopje to drop off a tool he had borrowed. The neighbor invited him in, and the neighbor’s wife came from the kitchen with cups.

“Coffee or tea?” she asked.

Aleksandar waved his hand. “No, no, don’t bother,” he said, voice clipped. “I’m not here for that.”

She hesitated, holding the tray. The neighbor said, “It’s already made, sit.”

Aleksandar sighed and sat, and the wife placed a cup in front of him anyway. Before she had even sat down, Aleksandar grabbed the cup and took a long drink. The liquid was hot, and he slurped loudly.

The wife was still pulling her chair out. “Careful, it’s hot,” she said.

Aleksandar set the cup down hard and said, “It’s fine,” then immediately picked it up again and drank another mouthful.

The neighbor started to speak, but Aleksandar interrupted with, “So, about that drill—” and gestured with the cup in the air. The wife finally sat with her own cup untouched, watching Aleksandar finish half of his.

“Do you want sugar?” she asked.

Aleksandar shrugged. “Whatever,” he said, and drank again before she could reach the sugar bowl.

He wiped his mouth with the back of his hand and leaned forward over the table, still holding the cup.
